name: Data sharing, interoperability, and cyber security anxiety
description: 41% cite cyber/data security costs as barrier, MODS mandatory by July 2026
severity_ranking: High and rising — 41% of providers in the 2025 DHSC technology survey cited cyber and data security costs as a barrier to technology adoption (behind set-up costs at 73% and licensing fees at 70%). The Minimum Operational Data Standard (MODS) becomes mandatory for all NHS Assured DSCR suppliers by 1 July 2026, with non-compliant suppliers losing their assured status. The Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 introduces statutory digital information standards for health and social care IT systems. The Cyber Security and Resilience Bill will extend new requirements to organisations in the health and social care supply chain. This pain point is intensifying as regulatory deadlines approach.
audience_affected: CEO/owners and operations directors face strategic decisions about data governance, supplier compliance, and cyber insurance. Registered managers must ensure staff follow data protection protocols while maintaining care continuity. Compliance managers bear the burden of understanding overlapping regulations (GDPR, Data Security and Protection Toolkit, MODS, Cyber Essentials). IT staff (where they exist — many providers have no dedicated IT resource) must implement technical controls.
financial_impact: Cyber and data security costs were cited as a barrier by 41% of providers, with the cost sitting on top of already-stretched budgets for set-up (73% barrier) and licensing (70% barrier). The cost of Cyber Essentials certification alone is £300-£500+, with Cyber Essentials Plus running £1,500-£5,000+. A data breach or ransomware attack can cost a care provider £50,000-£500,000+ in recovery costs, regulatory fines, and reputational damage. Providers must also budget for staff training, updated policies, and potentially new or upgraded software to meet MODS and interoperability requirements. Failure to meet MODS by July 2026 could mean losing access to NHS Assured suppliers, forcing costly platform migration.
emotional_trigger: "We don't even understand what MODS is, let alone how to comply with it." "We're terrified of a cyber attack — we hold the most sensitive data about vulnerable people." "Every time we share data with the NHS, we worry about whether we're doing it right." "Our software supplier says they're compliant but we have no way to verify that." "We can barely afford the software — now we need to pay for cyber security on top?"
ai_solvability: Partially solvable. AI can help with: automated data quality checking against MODS standards, intelligent data mapping between different care record systems, real-time anomaly detection for cyber security threats, automated generation of Data Security and Protection Toolkit (DSPT) evidence, AI-assisted privacy impact assessments, and natural language summaries of complex regulatory requirements. However, the fundamental challenges of interoperability infrastructure, supplier compliance, and funding for security measures require systemic solutions beyond AI.
source_evidence: DHSC 2025 Adult Social Care Provider Technology Survey: 41% cite cyber/data security costs as barrier. Digitising Social Care programme: MODS mandatory for all assured DSCR suppliers by 1 July 2026. Care Control Systems: first NHS Assured DSCR to achieve MODS certification ahead of deadline. Data (Use and Access) Act 2025: Section 121 requires statutory digital information standards. Cyber Security and Resilience Bill: extends security requirements to health/social care supply chain. Digital Care Hub policy briefings on interoperability and MODS. HCPA policy briefing on interoperability in adult social care. NHS England Social Care Interoperability Platform development.
government_policy_driver: MODS (mandatory July 2026), Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 (statutory information standards), Cyber Security and Resilience Bill, Digitising Social Care programme, NHS Cyber Security Strategy to 2030, Data Security and Protection Toolkit (DSPT), Connecting Care Records Programme (ending 2026 — responsibility shifting to local systems).
competitor_exploitation: Care Control Systems is positioning itself as 'the first NHS Assured DSCR to achieve MODS certification ahead of July 2026 deadline' — using compliance as a competitive differentiator. Person Centred Software holds Cyber Essentials, GP Connect certification, and NHS Assured status. Nourish Care is ISO 27001:2022 and Cyber Essentials Plus compliant and was the first on the NHS Assured Solutions List. Log My Care publishes MODS explainer content ('MODS explained: The first real step towards joined-up social care data') as thought leadership.
content_format_fit: MODS compliance checklist (downloadable PDF), interactive timeline showing key regulatory deadlines (MODS July 2026, Cyber Security Bill, DSPT), jargon-busting glossary of data sharing terms, webinar series on preparing for MODS, data security readiness self-assessment tool.
objection_link: "Data sharing is too risky — we'd rather keep our records to ourselves." Counter: MODS compliance will be mandatory by July 2026 for any provider using an NHS Assured DSCR. The Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 creates a legal framework for safe data sharing. Not preparing now means a rushed, expensive transition later. AI tools can automate much of the compliance burden and actually improve data security through anomaly detection and access controls.
